solutions.solution: If L is a committee then M is also on that committee. This means that two members of the committee have already been assigned. O cannot be either K or Q or both. This one you may want to diagram: E FS G, Q NR O. L and M must be appointed either to the Entertainment Committee or to the Food Services Committee. Take the first alternative: E FS G, QLM N R. So, Q must be assigned to the Games Committee: E FS G, QLM KPQ. But now we cannot separate N and R. So we try the second alternative: E FS G, Q LM KPQ. N and R cannot serve on the same committee: E FS G, Q N/R LM N/R KPQ. So, J must be assigned to Entertainment Committee: J E FS G, Q N/R LM N/R KPQ. Again, we will use a diagram: E FS G, QL Q. Of course, M must serve with L, which means that K must serve with Q: E FS G, QLM QK JP. This means J must serve on the Games Committee; N and R must be appointed one to the Food services Committee and one to the Games Committee; and P is appointed to the Games Committee: E FS G, QLM QK JP, G N/R N/R. So, statements I and II are necessarily true, while III is only possibly true.

Enter the information on a diagram: E FS G, QL JP. QL can also be appointed to Entertainment (filling up that committee). So, M must be appointed to Games (J and K cannot be together): and K must be appointed to Games (to be with K), and N and R must be appointed to Games and the other to Food Services, with P being appointed to Food Services: E FS G, QLM JP KQ, G N/R N/R. So, only statement I is necessarily true.

Enter the information on a diagram: E FS G, QL JP. QL can also be appointed to Entertainment (filling up that committee). So, M must be appointed to Games (J and K cannot be together): and K must be appointed to Games (to be with K), and N and R must be appointed one to Games and the other to Food Services, with P being appointed to Food Services: E FS G, QLM JP KQ, G N/R N/R. So, only statement I is necessarily true.
